enrichment.summary: Kyle gives his take on maritime law following a viewer request, covering territorial water boundaries, ship registration (particularly Panama's dominance), the 2020 Beirut blast as a case of port negligence, the South China Sea dispute, and his opinion that the US should back off and let China develop those islands. He also discusses personal maritime life as an expression of self-sufficiency, and wanders into ancient maritime history covering Rome, the Punic Wars, and a Book of Kings legend connecting Irish origins to Scythia.
enrichment.speaker: kyle

stances.position: China has a more legitimate claim; it's their backyard and they have the resources to develop it

stances.topic: US military presence near South China Sea
stances.position: Childish and wasteful; the US should withdraw its bases and stop hovering
Those islands should be drilled because offshore extraction is environmentally preferable to Arctic or Gulf drilling
Government should actively encourage it as the most self-sufficient, cost-effective way to live and travel
Lebanon's fault for negligent storage of impounded cargo; nothing to do with maritime law
Reckless and provocative; the US should close foreign bases instead
Society should eliminate middlemen wherever possible; self-sufficiency (bikes, boats, backpacking) is the ideal

Um, uh, but then in like the year 1900, they revamped all that. Cannons could shoot longer and they got more, um, you know, sophisticated.
So they decided that anything that was 12 miles out from shoreline was, um, belonged to the country.
